title: Cat Straining and Crying in the Litter Box: Is It an Emergency?
meta_desc: If your cat is straining or crying in the litter box, they may have a life-threatening blockage. Learn why this requires immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Straining to urinate is a life-threatening medical emergency in cats that can lead to kidney failure within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat is unable to pass urine or is crying in the litter box.

Why is my cat straining and howling in the litter box?

  • Frequent litter box visits and forceful straining are signs of a serious urinary issue.
  • This behavior often indicates Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a dangerous urinary blockage.
  • Blockages occur when the urethra is obstructed by crystals, mucus, or stones, preventing the bladder from emptying.

Is my cat experiencing a life-threatening medical emergency?

  • Yes, a urinary blockage is a high-urgency emergency that is especially dangerous for male cats.
  • Failure to empty the bladder leads to a toxic buildup, kidney failure, and potential heart failure within 24 to 48 hours.
  • You must seek emergency veterinary care immediately if your cat is straining and producing little to no urine.

How can a photo of my cat’s litter box help the triage team?

  • A photo of the litter box or your cat's posture provides vital information to the veterinary team.
  • Showing a lack of urine or small, bloody spots helps confirm the severity of a potential obstruction.
  • This information allows the clinic to prioritize your cat for immediate stabilization upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Straining and crying in the litter box in cats can be indicative of feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) and may be an emergency. Potential underlying causes include urethral obstruction (often due to matrix-crystalline plugs), urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, or urolithiasis. Feline interstitial cystitis should also be considered, diagnosed by excluding other causes of lower urinary tract disease through diagnostics such as radiographs, ultrasonography, urinalysis, urine culture, and cystoscopy. Prompt veterinary attention is crucial to determine the underlying cause and initiate appropriate treatment.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1573)

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if it passes?
faqs.answer: No. Urinary obstructions are time-sensitive. Waiting even a few hours can lead to irreversible organ damage or death. Immediate professional intervention is required.

faqs.question: Why are male cats more at risk?
faqs.answer: Male cats have a much longer and narrower urethra than female cats, which makes it far easier for small stones or inflammatory debris to cause a total blockage.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of a urinary blockage?
faqs.answer: Other symptoms include lethargy, loss of appetite, vomiting, hiding, and excessive grooming of the genital area.
